question_title: Will any poll of the 2026 California gubernatorial primary released between May 11 and May 17, 2026 show two Republicans holding the top two spots?
summary: This question requires two things: (1) at least one qualifying poll released May 11-17, and (2) that poll showing two Republicans (Hilton and Bianco) in the top two. Component 1 is likely (~85%) given active pre-primary polling season. Component 2 is unlikely (~9-15% per poll) because post-Swalwell Democratic consolidation has pushed Steyer and Becerra ahead of Bianco. None of the 7 most recent polls show 2 Rs clearly in top 2. Monte Carlo simulation using realistic polling uncertainty (SD ~2.1pp per candidate) yields ~9.3% per poll. Combined with poll release probability and the possibility of multiple polls, the overall probability is approximately 13%.

factors.description: Bianco (R) averages ~13.2%, trailing both Steyer (D, ~15.4%) and Becerra (D, ~14.7%) by 1.5-2pp. He needs to exceed ALL Democrats to qualify.
Zero of 7 recent polls (post-Swalwell exit, April-May) show two Republicans in top 2. Trend has shifted away from 2-R scenario as Democratic field consolidated.
High undecided share (14-26%) creates poll-to-poll volatility, giving Bianco a nonzero chance of jumping ahead in any single poll.
Multiple polls likely released in the May 11-17 window (2-3 expected given pre-primary frequency), increasing chances that at least one catches a 2-R scenario.

Emerson College pollster has historically shown higher Bianco numbers (14% vs 10-12% in other polls) and is likely to release in mid-May. However even their last poll showed Bianco tied with Steyer, not ahead.
~15-20% probability that NO qualifying polls are released in this specific 7-day window, which would resolve NO automatically.

reasoning:
## Forecast: **13% probability (YES)**

### Key Analysis

**Two components must both occur:**

1. **At least one qualifying poll released May 11-17 (~85% likely):** We're in prime pre-primary polling season with Emerson, PPIC, SurveyUSA, and others all likely to release. Monthly cadences align well with this window.

2. **That poll shows two Republicans in top 2 (~9-15% per poll):** This is the hard part. Since Swalwell's exit on April 12, Democratic support has consolidated around Steyer (~15.4%) and Becerra (~14.7%), both now ahead of Bianco (~13.2%). **Zero of the 7 most recent polls** show two Republicans clearly in the top two.

### Monte Carlo Results
- Simulated 100,000 polls with realistic uncertainty (SD ≈ 2.1pp per candidate)
- **9.3% chance any single poll shows 2 Rs in top 2** at current average support levels
- With 1-3 polls expected in the window, combined probability ≈ **13%**

### Why not higher?
- Bianco trails the leading Democrats by ~2pp — within MOE but on the wrong side
- The post-Swalwell trend has moved decisively *away* from the 2-R scenario
- Even the Emerson poll (most favorable to Bianco historically) showed him only tied with Steyer last time

### Why not lower?
- High undecided share (14-26%) creates meaningful poll-to-poll volatility
- Multiple polls in the window give multiple chances
- Some pollsters (Emerson, Berkeley IGS) have house effects favoring higher Bianco numbers
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will any poll of the 2026 California gubernatorial primary released between May 11 and May 17, 2026 show two Republicans holding the top two spots?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43429)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if at least one publicly released poll of the 2026 California gubernatorial primary (general primary ballot, all candidates / all voters — not a party-subset crosstab) with a field period ending on or after May 1, 2026 is first publicly released between 00:00 UTC on May 11, 2026 and 23:59 UTC on May 17, 2026, and shows two candidates affiliated with the Republican Party occupying the top two positions by support among likely or registered voters (a tie between two Republicans for first or second, with no non-Republican tied with or ahead of them in the top two, also counts). Otherwise it resolves NO. The polling table on the Wikipedia article "2026 California gubernatorial election" (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_California_gubernatorial_election#Polling) will be used as the primary resolution source. If that table is unavailable or incomplete, polls reported by [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) released in the same window may be used.
fine_print:
- Only polls of the top-line all-candidate primary ballot count; head-to-head matchups, party-only subsamples, and hypothetical general-election pairings are excluded.
- "Republican" means a candidate listed as affiliated with the Republican Party on the California Secretary of State's certified list of candidates (e.g., Steve Hilton, Chad Bianco). Independents and minor-party candidates do not count as Republicans.
- The "release date" is the date the poll's results are first made publicly available (e.g., posted on the pollster's site or first reported by a news outlet), not the field period.
- A poll where a Republican is tied with a Democrat for second place does NOT qualify, since a Democrat would also occupy the top two.
- Internal/partisan polls released by a campaign, party committee, or affiliated PAC do not count; only polls conducted by independent polling organizations or media-sponsored polls count.
- If multiple qualifying polls are released in the window, only one is needed to resolve YES.

factors.description: Bianco (R) averages ~13.2%, trailing both Steyer (D, ~15.4%) and Becerra (D, ~14.7%) by 1.5-2pp. He needs to exceed ALL Democrats to qualify.
Zero of 7 recent polls (post-Swalwell exit, April-May) show two Republicans in top 2. Trend has shifted away from 2-R scenario as Democratic field consolidated.
High undecided share (14-26%) creates poll-to-poll volatility, giving Bianco a nonzero chance of jumping ahead in any single poll.
Multiple polls likely released in the May 11-17 window (2-3 expected given pre-primary frequency), increasing chances that at least one catches a 2-R scenario.

Emerson College pollster has historically shown higher Bianco numbers (14% vs 10-12% in other polls) and is likely to release in mid-May. However even their last poll showed Bianco tied with Steyer, not ahead.
There's a ~15-20% probability that NO qualifying polls are released in this specific 7-day window, which would resolve NO automatically.

input.anchor: Base rate derived from Monte Carlo simulation: ~9.3% chance any given poll shows 2 Rs in top 2, combined with ~85% chance of at least one poll being released = ~13% overall.
input.assessment:
This question requires two things to happen: (1) at least one qualifying poll released in the May 11-17 window, and (2) that poll showing two Republicans (Hilton and Bianco) in the top two positions. 

Component 1 is likely (~85%) given that we're in the prime pre-primary polling season with multiple active pollsters.

Component 2 is unlikely (~9-15% per poll) because the post-Swalwell consolidation of Democratic support has pushed both Steyer and Becerra ahead of Bianco in polling averages. Bianco trails the leading Democrat by ~2pp, which is within polling MOE but on the wrong side of the threshold. None of the 7 most recent polls show 2 Rs clearly in top 2.

The combined probability is approximately 13%.
input.calibration_notes: The Monte Carlo simulation uses realistic polling uncertainty (SD ~2.1pp per candidate combining true support uncertainty and sampling noise). The simulation's per-poll probability of 9.3% is consistent with the empirical observation that 0 of 7 recent polls showed 2 Rs (binomial probability of 0/7 given p=0.093 is about 50%, quite plausible). The anchor is data-driven rather than intuitive.
input.key_uncertainties: The biggest uncertainty is whether Bianco's true support is higher or lower than the average suggests. Some pollsters (Emerson, Berkeley IGS) have consistently shown him higher, while others (CBS/YouGov, SurveyUSA) show him lower. If his true support is at 14-15% rather than 13%, the per-poll probability roughly doubles.
input.tool_audit: Research tool provided comprehensive polling data and pollster cadence info - very useful. Monte Carlo simulation via execute_code was essential for quantifying the per-poll probability. Coherence links returned empty. Metaculus question details were helpful for parsing resolution criteria.
input.update_triggers: A major Democratic candidate dropping out before polls are conducted would dramatically reduce P(2 Rs). Conversely, a Democratic scandal or a new Bianco endorsement could increase it. If we learn that Emerson or Berkeley IGS specifically are releasing a poll this week, that slightly increases the probability.
input.process_reflection: The research sub-agent was excellent here - it retrieved comprehensive polling data, aggregator averages, and pollster cadence information in a single call. The Monte Carlo simulation approach worked well for this threshold-crossing question. The main friction was in carefully parsing the tie rules in the resolution criteria, which required close reading.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

**Resolution criteria**: Properly fetched via get_metaculus_questions and correctly parsed. The agent correctly handled the tie rule (R tied with D for 2nd doesn't qualify) and the partisan poll exclusion. ✅

**Factor 1 (Bianco trails ~2pp, logit=-1.2)**: Verified against trace. Research data shows Bianco at ~13.2% across 5 aggregators, Steyer at ~15.4%, Becerra at ~14.7%. All numbers match trace sources (270toWin, DDHQ, Race to WH, RCP, FiftyPlusOne). ✅

**Factor 2 (0/7 recent polls show 2 Rs, logit=-1.0)**: Verified against trace. The code execution systematically checked all 7 recent polls and correctly found 0 qualifying. The Emerson poll (Bianco 14% tied with Steyer 14%) was correctly excluded per the tie rule. ✅

**Factor 3 (High undecided 14-26%, logit=+0.5)**: Verified. CBS/YouGov shows 26% undecided, Evitarus shows 14%, both confirmed in trace. Direction correct — more undecided = more volatility = slightly more chance of a 2-R result. ✅

**Factor 4 (Multiple polls expected, logit=+0.3)**: Verified. Research shows Emerson (monthly cadence, last April 14-15), PPIC (monthly, publishes mid-month), and others expected. Direction correct. ✅

**Factor 5 (Emerson house effect, logit=+0.2)**: Verified. Emerson showed Bianco at 14% vs CBS/YouGov's 10% and SurveyUSA's 12%. But agent correctly notes even Emerson's last poll had Bianco tied with Steyer, not ahead. Low magnitude appropriate. ✅

**Factor 6 (Chance of no polls, logit=-0.3)**: Agent's estimate, consistent with their simulation analysis. ✅

**Double-counting**: Factors 1 and 2 draw on overlapping evidence (the same polling data showing Bianco trails). Factors 4 and 6 are two sides of the same coin (poll release probability). The agent explicitly acknowledged this in the reflection, noting the factor-implied probability (~20.5%) diverges from the Monte Carlo estimate (13%), and correctly chose the simulation result over naive factor arithmetic. The agent's awareness mitigates the concern.

**Monte Carlo simulation**: The simulation uses SD=2.1pp per candidate, which is consistent with observed poll-to-poll variation (Bianco 10-16%, Steyer 10-18%). The 9.3% per-poll probability is computationally derived and the agent verified it against the empirical base rate (0/7 ≈ 50% likely under p=0.093, which is plausible).

**Probability assessment**: 13% is on the conservative end of the 13-16% range I'd estimate, but defensible. The Monte Carlo Version 1 gives 12.4%, Version 2 gives 15.3%. The agent chose 13%, splitting slightly low. I would put this at perhaps 14-15%, accounting for the higher poll frequency in pre-primary season, but the difference is not meaningful (1-2pp).

**Counterargument**: Reasonably strong — identifies Emerson house effect, wider tails, and Berkeley IGS history as specific mechanisms for YES. Not a token gesture. ✅

**Self-confidence**: 0.45 is appropriate given uncertainty about pollster release timing and Bianco's true support level. ✅

**Tool audit**: Consistent with metrics (8 calls, 0 errors). ✅

No hallucinated evidence, no wrong-direction factors, no contradictory assessment, no resolution misalignment, no missing criteria recovery issues. The forecast is well-grounded in trace evidence.
